agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider 2025 Homeless Count report for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council received the 2025 Homeless Count report for information. The March 11, 2025 point-in-time count identified 2,715 homeless individuals in Vancouver (1,952 sheltered, 763 unsheltered), a 12% increase from 2023. 79% of respondents reported being homeless for at least a year. Indigenous peoples are 18 times more likely to be homeless than non-Indigenous peoples, making up 42% of respondents. Black and African diaspora peoples make up 9% of respondents but only 1% of Vancouver's population. No bylaw or rule change was voted on.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants totaling $5,509,770 to 85 organizations. Grants include $2,828,160 for five Institutional Operating grants, $709,760 for 26 Annual Operating grants, $1,897,550/year for three years (2026-2028) for 52 Multi-year Operating grants, a $75,000 additional Making Space grant to Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society, and a $728,000 one-time grant to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades. Recommendations A-C, E, and G require a two-thirds majority vote under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on April 14, 2026 — if approved, these grants could affect funding for arts groups your business partners with.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award 19 grants totalling $89,000 to community sport events in Vancouver during Spring 2026, as part of the Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant program. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. If approved, the grants would help local organizations host sporting events in the city.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council decision — this could mean funding opportunities for your Spring 2026 event.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
Recommendation A requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is being asked to approve a 2026 general purpose tax distribution of approximately 57.5% residential and 42.5% non-residential, with no tax shift proposed. The total general purposes tax levy is $1,224,250,000. Tax rates will be adjusted for assessment market changes to ensure revenue neutrality. The recommendation maintains the existing tax share split from 2025.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — a change in tax distribution could directly affect your property tax bill.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026 property tax distribution

agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
4a. Budget Resolution
4b. Report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council received the 2025 Property Tax Exemptions report. In 2025, $60.9 billion of properties (12% of the Assessment Roll) were exempt, resulting in $335.1 million in forgone general purpose taxes, partially offset by $34.8 million in compensating payments-in-lieu of taxes. Any net forgone taxes are redistributed to other taxpayers. The report outlines statutory exemptions (e.g., Crown lands, charities, hospitals, churches) and permissive exemptions Council may enact by by-law.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy that would formalize the City's role in coastal flood management. The policy leverages land-use authority and redevelopment opportunities to incrementally improve long-term flood resilience. City-built structural flood measures outside redevelopment contexts will be considered only in specific situations, focused on preserving City facilities and core service delivery, subject to financial capacity. The policy does not change existing flood construction levels (minimum 4.6 m) or the expanded floodplain area adopted in 2014.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and prepare to comment at the public hearing.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Insurance & Finance
agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Vancouver Heritage Foundation annual report and special voting rule
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on approving the Vancouver Heritage Foundation's 2025 Annual Report and a $152,375 operating grant for 2026, the first year of a three-year agreement (2026-2028). Approval requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. The grant funds staffing, educational programming, and conservation work.

key_dates.label: Council vote on VHF grant and annual report

agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on 2026–2029 public plaza drinking sites
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposed 3-year program (2026–2029) allowing alcohol consumption at 13 public plazas, plus a 2-year extension for the Drinker's Lounge parklet at 111 Princess St. The program transitions from annual to a 3-year cycle. Permissible hours align with nearby washroom operating hours. All proposed sites have access to washrooms (portable or nearby). No health or safety issues have been reported since the pilot began in 2020.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If your business is near a proposed plaza site, monitor the council decision on April 14, 2026, for potential impacts on foot traffic, noise, or licensing conditions.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026–2029 plaza alcohol program

agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on endorsing a $200,000 grant application to the Union of BC Municipalities Local Government Development Approvals Program for the Vancouver Housing Service Redesign project. If approved, the grant would fund a cross-department initiative to streamline approvals for large-scale housing developments by simplifying regulations, creating reusable guides and templates, and triaging applications by complexity. The grant covers 100% of eligible costs.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Vancouver.

key_dates.label: Council vote on grant application — Vancouver

agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on 11 housing rezoning condition changes for specific properties, including 1434–1456 West 8th Avenue, 2090 West Broadway, 2111 Main Street, and others. The changes are part of the Rental Development Relief Program (RDRP), a two-year pilot (Feb 1, 2026 – Dec 15, 2027) that allows eligible mid- and high-rise BMR projects to adjust affordability levels from a 20% discount to CMHC city-wide average rents, to rates not exceeding city-wide averages for all purpose-built rental apartments. Eligible sites must have fewer than 10 existing purpose-built rental units, be under 8,000 sq m, and not yet have a Stage 1 building permit (with limited exceptions for projects permitted Dec 15, 2025 – Mar 20, 2026).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the full agenda report and Appendix A to see if your property or project is affected by the 11 specific rezoning condition changes.

key_dates.label: Rental Development Relief Program pilot ends

agenda_briefs.source_body:
10.	Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The amendment would change the site from for-profit affordable rental housing (with 30% of units for households below HILs, rent capped at 30% of income) to 100% secured market rental housing, and increase the floor space ratio (FSR) from 3.49 to 3.5. The decision has not been made yet — it is up for approval at a future council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect development rules for properties in this area.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone 4088 Granville Street from FSD (First Shaughnessy) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. The proposal permits three three-storey residential rental buildings with 31 units, a maximum floor space ratio (FSR) of 1.2, and a maximum building height of 13.4 m (44 ft.). At least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms. The site is 1,858.8 m².
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this rezoning could affect nearby property values or development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The plan would change the land use rules (CD-1 rezoning) to allow a six-storey mixed-use building with 82 secured market rental units and commercial at grade. A key condition: if a stage one building permit is not issued within two years of approval, at least 20% of residential floor area must revert to below-market rental units. The draft bylaw also sets a maximum floor space ratio of 3.50, a minimum of 594.9 m² of commercial space, and a maximum building height of 22 m.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the two-year deadline for issuing a stage one building permit to avoid triggering the below-market rental requirement.

key_dates.label: Two-year deadline to issue stage one building permit before BMR requirement triggers

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 310 East 14th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposed bylaw to rezone 310 East 14th Avenue from R3-3 to CD-1, allowing a 20-storey, 138-unit rental building with 20% of floor area as below-market rental units. The maximum FSR would increase from 2.70 to 6.53, and building height from 23 m to 60 m. The site is under the Broadway Plan and existing tenants are covered by the Tenant Relocation and Protection Policy.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit written comments to Vancouver City Council before the vote.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue from R3-3 to R5-4 District. If approved, the rezoning would allow a 20-storey residential building with 172 rental units, with 20% of residential floor area secured as below-market rental units. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge bylaw and density bonus updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council directed staff to consult on proposed Amenity Cost Charge (ACC) bylaw and Bill 16 density bonus/inclusionary zoning updates. The ACC bylaw is expected for Council consideration on July 28, 2026. Bill 16 updates to the Zoning and Development Bylaw are anticipated in a referral report on May 5, 2026. A workshop with development industry stakeholders on the ACC bylaw is planned for May 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for public consultation opportunities and the May 2026 industry workshop on the proposed ACC bylaw.

key_dates.label: Bill 16 bylaw updates referral report to Council

agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property for Trounce Alley & Blood Alley Square maintenance costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. This is called a 'local improvement' — a way for the city to pay for upgrades or upkeep in a specific area by billing the properties that benefit from them. If approved, the bylaw would add a special charge to the property taxes of affected landowners to cover things like cleaning, repairs, and other upkeep for the alleys and square.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own property in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square area, watch for the council vote — this could add a new charge to your property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property costs for East Hastings parking project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge certain property owners in the East Hastings area for the costs of a shared parking project in 2025. The bylaw would 'assess real property' — meaning it would add a special fee or tax on specific properties to pay for the collective parking project. This is not yet approved; it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending to voice your support or concerns about the proposed parking assessment.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in how the building will be used — in this case, to ensure the units remain as affordable rental housing for a set number of years. This is a proposed decision only; it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amend housing agreement for Prior Street, Malkin Avenue, Station Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change a Housing Agreement for three properties in Burnaby: 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules for how the housing is used — often to keep rents affordable or ensure certain types of housing. This amendment would update the terms of that existing agreement. The decision has not been made yet; council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 5.	A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amend CD-1 (198) bylaw for 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would change the rules for the property at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. This property is currently zoned under a special 'Comprehensive Development' zone (CD-1 (198)). The amendment would alter the existing bylaw (No. 6254) that governs what can be built or done on this site. Because Councillor Montague was not present for earlier discussions, they must review the proceedings before they are allowed to vote on this proposal. The exact changes to the bylaw have not been detailed in this agenda item, but the decision could affect future development or use of this specific property.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue from C-3A to CD-1, allowing a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor commercial space. Maximum FSR would increase from 3.0 to 8.1, height from 9.2 m to 50.6 m. No public hearing is required as the proposal is consistent with the Vancouver Official Development Plan and Broadway Plan.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for Council decision on April 14, 2026 — approval would advance the rezoning to by-law enactment.

key_dates.label: Council decision on 888 West 8th Avenue CD-1 rezoning

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby mixed-use district
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'C-2A Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, the new rules would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels (under a certain size) more quickly, without needing a full rezoning process for each project. The proposal would rezone certain parcels from C-2, C-2B, C-2C and C-2C1 districts to the new C-2A district. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet. Council will consider the report at an upcoming meeting, and there may be a public hearing before a final vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to tell Council how the new district could affect your business or property.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

Referral Report
